coredata.dc:title: High doses of graphene quantum dots impacts on microcirculation system: An observational study
coredata.prism:publicationName: European Journal of Pharmaceutics and Biopharmaceutics
coredata.dc:creator: Barros, Aline Oliveira da Silva
Ricci-Junior, Eduardo
Pereira, Jonathas Xavier
Pikula, Konstantin
Golokhvast, Kirill
Manahães, Alex Christian
Souza, Pedro Filho Noronha
Alencar, Luciana Magalhães Rebelo
Bouskela, Eliete
Santos-Oliveira, Ralph
coredata.dc:description: Graphene and its derivatives are in the edge of technology with a wide and diverse range of applications. In the last years, especially graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have had their biomedical application expanded in scope, mainly focused on cancer therapy, drug delivery and imaging. Although many studies have evaluated the application of this nanomaterial in biomedical field, only a few studies aimed to understand their biological impact in human health. In this regard, here we evaluated the impact of high doses of GQDs on the microcirculation of a healthy animal model to better assess risks of its use in humans. Our data show that successive applications of GQDs cause irreversible damage to the microcirculation. After seven days, a complete destruction of the microcirculation has been observed. In addition, GQDs showed substantial activity in human erythrocytes. Our findings suggest that risks associated with the use of GQDs, as well as all graphene derivatives, must be better understood, especially concerning biomedical application. A greater understanding of how GQDs impact body circulation, including the context of environmental and engineered nanosystems, is of paramount importance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Circulatory system
Microcirculation
Toxicity
Preclinical study

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by Carlos Chagas Filho Foundation for Research Support of Rio de Janeiro State (FAPERJ) (Cientista do Nosso Estado: E-26/200.815/2021; Rede NanoSaude: E-26/0 https://doi.org/10.000981/2019 , Pesquisa na UEZO: E-26/0 https://doi.org/10.002362/2019 ; Temáticos: E-26/211.269/2021, Infraestrutura e Pesquisa na UEZO e UERJ: E-26//211.207/2021, Bolsa de Pós Doutorado Senior (PDS): E-26/202.320/2021) CNPq (Bolsa de Produtividade 1B: 301069/2018-2) to Ralph Santos-Oliveira.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Atomic Force microscopy (A), dynamic light scattering (b), Raman (c) and XRD analysis (d) of GQDs.

Morphometric analysis of venules in animals exposed to graphene quantum dots at concentrations of 0.5 or 1.0 g/mg of body mass. Due to the destructive effect of GQDs on the microcirculation, venular diameters could no longer be measured in GQDs-exposed animals (N = 4).

Morphometric analysis of arterioles in animals exposed to graphene quantum dots at concentrations of 0.5 and 1.0 g/Kg of body mass. Due to the destructive effect of GQDs on the microcirculation, arteriolar diameters could no longer be measured in GQDs-exposed animals (N = 4).

Dorsal view of animals topically exposed to graphene quantum dots. In A is the control group, where it is possible to observe microcirculatory integrity. In B, animals were exposed to graphene quantum dots (0.5 g/Kg): A total loss of integrity is observed. The same in C, where animals were exposed to graphene quantum dots at a higher dose (1 g/Kg).

Photomicrographs of skin and carotid arteries. Representative images of skin tissue obtained from the control group (A) and from the topical treatment with 0.5 g/Kg of GQDs(B) or 1 g/Kg of GQDs(C). Fragment in cross-section of carotid arteries from the control group (D) and from the group treated with intraperitoneal GQDs at 1 g/Kg (E). Black arrows indicate the presence of inflammatory infiltrate (panniculitis). Red arrows indicate the intimal layer of carotid arteries. Data are representative of four independent experiments. Magnification: A-E (100x).
Effect of GQDs (5 mg mL−1) on human red blood cells type-A (A), type-B (B) and type-O (C).
The pattern of the substance identifying the presence of citrate in the graphene oxide sample due the fabrication route used.

head.ce:title: High doses of graphene quantum dots impacts on microcirculation system: An observational study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene and its derivatives are in the edge of technology with a wide and diverse range of applications. In the last years, especially graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have had their biomedical application expanded in scope, mainly focused on cancer therapy, drug delivery and imaging. Although many studies have evaluated the application of this nanomaterial in biomedical field, only a few studies aimed to understand their biological impact in human health. In this regard, here we evaluated the impact of high doses of GQDs on the microcirculation of a healthy animal model to better assess risks of its use in humans. Our data show that successive applications of GQDs cause irreversible damage to the microcirculation. After seven days, a complete destruction of the microcirculation has been observed. In addition, GQDs showed substantial activity in human erythrocytes. Our findings suggest that risks associated with the use of GQDs, as well as all graphene derivatives, must be better understood, especially concerning biomedical application. A greater understanding of how GQDs impact body circulation, including the context of environmental and engineered nanosystems, is of paramount importance.

ce:section.ce:para: The microcirculation status impacts several clinical outcomes, since it is responsible for the supply of oxygen and substrates to the tissues, in addition to playing an important role in body clearance, removing by-products of cellular metabolism. It is formed primarily by vessels, arterioles, metarterioles, capillaries and venules with diameters less than 100 µm, corresponding to 350 m2 in area, constituting the largest endothelial surface of the body [1–4]. Among the structural components that form the microcirculation, the arteriole stands out due to its predominant function, as it is responsible for the pressure gradient between the distal and proximal capillaries, thus promoting blood flow control [5–11].

In the last few decades, the use of nanoparticles has been increasing exponentially [12–14]. The main applications of nanoparticles include delivering diagnostic and therapeutic agents by several different vias: Cutaneous [15–17], intraperitoneal [18–19], intravenous [20], intraarterial [21–22], pulmonary [23] and ocular [24]. In this regard, some benefits associated with the use of nanoparticles have been identified, such as: higher targeting, fewer adverse effects, and lower doses [25–26]. Among the considerable number of nanoparticles in use nowadays, graphene (and its derivatives) is one of the most used. For instance, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) associated with imatinib have been used for the treatment of leukemia [27] with good results [28,30].

The interaction of GQDs with biological systems depends of several aspects. For instance, due to its small size, GQDs may have changes in biocomaptibility and toxicty, when compared to graphene or graphene oxide [31]. Another important point is the presence of reactive groups on the GQDs surface, allowing the functionalization with various molecules and compounds, make them ideal candidates for cancer diagnosis and treatment [32]. Regarding the size, is important to notice that although GQDs are traditionally described as nanomaterials with size lower than 100 nm, for some clinical applications, this size must different. For example, focusing cancer application the fenestration issue must be considered, since functional pore may reach sizes as large as 2 μm in tumor vessels [33]. Also, once circulating nanoparticles extravasate through these large vascular pores [34]. Regarding the circulatory system, although must of vessels shows a fenestra varying from 5 to 12 nm, important subjacent circulatory system shows different fenestrae. For instance, hepatic sinusoidal blood capillary has pore size of 180 nm [35].

In most cases, the first and, usually, main interaction of these nanoparticles occur with the blood and blood vessels, through which they reach their targets: cell or tissues [36]. Although several studies have extensively evaluated the impact of nanoparticles in human cells (both healthy and cancerous) [37], very few studies have been performed that aimed to clearly understand the impact of the administration of nanoparticles in the body microcirculation. Understanding these interactions will facilitate the design of safe and effective nanoparticles for nanomedicine, suppressing unwanted effects such as disruptive interactions with blood proteins, cardiotoxicity, and others.

In the present study we evaluated the effects of graphene quantum dots exposure on the microcirculation in vivo by assessing the following parameters: venular and arteriolar diameters and functional capillary density. A histological analysis of the microcirculation was also carried out.

The GQDs synthesis and characterization were extensively described by Menezes et al. [38] and Haixia et al. [39]. In brief, the GQDs was produced by an electrochemical green route, using graphite rod and platinum, respectively anode and cathode, in a buffer solution of citric acid/sodium citrate (Sigma–Aldrich, USA). The electrolyte used was prepared by mixing 63.5 mL of 0.2 M citric acid with 36.5 mL of 0.2 M sodium citrate to obtain 100 mL of electrolyte. The electrochemical process was conducted at a constant current of 190 mA for 24 h using an ICEL PS-1500 adjustable power supply. After the oxidation electrolysis, the dispersion obtained was filtered to remove large particles. The filtered suspension was dried at 60 °C to reduce the volume of the dispersion to 10 mL. Finally, 50 mL of ethanol was added to the dispersion, and the upper phase corresponding to purified GQDs were collected and dried at 60 °C and stored.

The characterization has been performed by Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM), Raman Spectroscopy, X-Ray diffractometry (XRD) and dynamic light scattering (DLS). For the AFM analysis, the sample of GQDs were dissolved in distilled water. Then, 10 µL of the solution was dropped in fresh cleaved mica and dried at room temperature. The measurements were made using a Multimode 8 microscope (Bruker, Santa Barbara, CA) in QNM (Quantitative Nanomechanics) mode using probes model Scanasyst Air, with nominal spring constant of 0.4 N/m and nominal tip radio of 2 nm approximately. To evaluate the particle section, the analyses were performed with NanoScope Analysis 1.50 software and the images resolution were 256 samples per lines. The size determination was performed by dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a Zetasizer Nano ZS equipment (Malvern Instruments, UK). All measurements were performed in triplicate at 25 °C with a laser incidence angle relative to the sample of 173° using a 12-mm2 quartz cuvette. The Raman spectra were obtained using a Horiba-Jobin-Yvon triple spectrometer (model T64000). The instrument was equipped with a confocal attachment and a liquid-N2-cooled charge coupled device (CCD) detector. The excitation was produced using a green line (532.0 nm) from a solid-state laser (LAS-532–100-HREV) operating at 14 mW. The light was focused on the sample using an Olympus microscope with an MPLN 100 m objective lens. The spot of the scattered light was limited by the confocal attachment. The spectra were acquired in two ways: (i) at different points on the sample surface and (ii) by varying the position along the vertical direction centered on a particle. The graphene quantum dots were characterised by X-ray diffraction (XRD), analysis using Bruker AXS D8 Advance diffractometer equipped with CuKα radiation and working at 40 mA,/40 kV, performed at high angle (2θ = 15–68°) and low angle range (2θ = 1.3–8.3°). The final product of this process was the GQDs used in this study.

Experiments were performed on male Syrian golden hamsters (Mesocricetus auratus, ANILAB, Paulínea, SP, Brazil), n = 24 (6 animals per group), weighting between 95 and 105 g. Animals were housed one per cage under controlled conditions of luminosity (12:12 h light and dark cycle) and temperature (21.0 ± 1.0 °C), with free access to water and standard chow. All procedures were approved by the State University of Rio de Janeiro Animal Care and Use Committee (Rio de Janeiro, RJ, Brazil; protocol number CEUA/021/2015), which is consistent with United States National Institute of Health Guide for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals (National Research Council, 1996).

Animals were anesthetized by an intraperitoneal injection (ketamine 100 mg·kg −1 and xylazine 20 mg·kg −1), followed by shaving of the dorsal hair with a commercial hair-removing solution. For skinfold preparation, the dorsal skin of the back was elevated. To support the skinfold (or window chamber), two titanium frames with 12 mm circular openings were implanted. In brief, one of the frames was sutured to the skin and formed one side of the skinfold, the opposite skin layer was removed following the outline of the titanium frame circular opening, leaving only a thin layer of retractor muscle, connective tissue, and intact skin (towards the opposing side). Normal physiological saline was used for the prevention of dryness, and the wound was sealed with a cover glass, which was held by the other frame.

The animals were divided into five groups: group 1: animals that received intraperitoneally 1 g/Kg BM (body mass) of GQDs (n = 3); group 2: animals that received topically 0.5 g/Kg BM of GQDs (n = 6); group 3: animals that received topically 1 g/Kg BM of GQDs (n = 6); group 4: control group (n = 6). The animals from group 1 received the GQDs doses for 7 days consecutively. The animals from groups 2 and 3 received a single dose on the dorsal region on the first day of the experiment. The control group received just saline for 7 days consecutively. Animals were observed daily during the entire experimental period. After chamber implantation, animals were returned to their home cages. During surgery, a heating pad was kept near the animals, controlled by a rectal thermistor (LB750, Uppsala Process data AB, Sweden), to maintain their body temperature.

The unanesthetized animal was positioned in a restraining plexiglass tube attached to the stage of an intravital microscope (Ortholux, Leitz, Wetzlar, Germany) equipped with an epifluorescence assembly (100-W HBO mercury lamp with filter blocks, Leitz, Wetzlar, Germany). The microcirculation images were obtained using a Nikon 20x objective (Nikon Instruments, Japan) and a charge-coupled device digital video camera system (SBC-320P B/W Camera, Samsung, South Korea), which resulted in an 800-fold magnification at the video monitor. The acquired images of the microcirculation were recorded in digital media and analyzed off-line using Cap-Image 7.2 software (Dr.Zeintl, Biomedical Engineering, Germany) by an investigator blinded to GQDs treatment. According to a predefined protocol, 2 venules, 2 arterioles, and 10 capillary fields were selected in each animal. The same vessels and microscopic fields were studied throughout the experiment. Functional capillary density (FCD) was considered to be the total length (in millimeters) of spontaneously red blood cell (RBC)-perfused capillaries per square centimeter of tissue surface area (mm·cm −2).

Skin and carotid arteries fragments of both hamster groups were collected and immediately fixated in 4% buffered paraformaldehyde pH 7.4 for 24 h at 4 °C. Samples were dehydrated in growing concentrations of ethanol (70–100%) for 30 min each and washed 3 times in xylene for 30 min. Then, the samples were individually embedded in paraffin, sectioned into 4 μm thick slices and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). Bright-field pictures were acquired using the LAS X Core Leica Microsystems Software, Germany.

The hemolytic activity of GQDs was tested against all types (A, B, and O) of human red blood cells (HRBC), according to Oliveira et al [40]. Three types of HRBCs were chosen to understand the hemolytic effect of the GQDs on different blood types. The HRBCs from A, B and O were provided by the Ceará Hematology and Hemotherapy Center (Brazil).

The blood was collected in the presence of heparin (5 IU/mL) from anonymous healthy donors. The HRB cells were collected by centrifugation at 300 g for 5 min at 4 °C (Eppendorf Centrifuge 5418/5418R), and dissolved in sterile 0.15 M NaCl. After washing three times with 0.15 M NaCl, HRBCs were diluted to a working concentration of 2.5% in 0.15 M NaCl. Then, an aliquot of 100 μL was incubated with GQDs at 5 mg mL−1 or its vehicle 5% DMSO, individually, and then incubated for 30 min at 37 °C, followed by centrifugation (300 g for 5 min at 4 °C). Afterwards, the supernatants were meticulously collected and transferred to 96-well microtiter plates. Hemolysis (%) was calculated by measuring the presence of hemoglobin in the supernatant at 414 nm using an automated absorbance microplate reader. Negative (0%) and positive (100%) hemolysis were determined by treating HRBCs with 5% DMSO and 0.1% (v/v) Triton X-100, respectively. The hemolysis was calculated as described by Oliveira et al [40].

The results from animals that received either topical or intraperitoneal GQDs are shown on Figs. 2–5
. It is possible to observe that successive applications of graphene quantum dots in both concentrations (1.0 g/Kg and 0.5 g/Kg) by both vias (topical and intraperitoneal) resulted in the loss of integrity of venules, arterioles, and capillaries, culminating in total destruction of all microcirculatory network after the 7-day period of the experiment.

The parameters evaluated in the study (venular diameter, arteriolar diameter, and functional capillary density) clearly evidenced the destructive effect of GQDs on the microcirculation (Figs. 2 and 3). In fact, after the seven-day period, none of the selected parameters could be measured due the extent of the damage caused to the microcirculation by the nanoparticles.

The GQDs presented a mild hemolytic activity against human blood erythrocytes tested (Fig. 6 ) at a concentration of 5 mg mL−1. GQDs presented 26, 38, and 43% of hemolysis, respectively to type-A, type-B and type-O.

In order to evaluate the effects of high doses of graphene quantum dots on the microcirculation we used the intravital microscopy assay. This technique allows an accurate assessment of vascular morphology, through the analysis of venular and arteriolar diameters and functional capillary density.

Our data indicate that high concentrations of GQDs causes severe damage to the microcirculation in this animal model. This can be partially explained by the findings of Toy and collaborators [41]. According to these authors, who have used a microchannel setup and evaluated several nanoparticles, all nanoparticles suffer margination, which means a deposition in the vascular bed. According to Chiu and collaborators [42], who used a cremaster muscle preparation, a reduction in blood flow was observed 1 min post-injection as magnetic nanoparticles (5 mg/Kg) accumulated in the arteries. It is conceivable that a continuous i.p. administration of high doses of GQDs increase the margination process [43], culminating in the complete destruction of the microcirculation. Miller and collaborators [44], using human subjects to evaluate the translocation of gold nanoparticles (116 ± 12 μg/m3) from lung to circulation, observed that these nanoparticles preferentially accumulate in vascular inflammation sites after the translocation process, with an expressive accumulation in the aortic arch.

Regarding the total loss of microcirculation integrity in animals that received topical GQD, we hypothesize that massive amounts of GQDs penetrated the skin and caused the destruction. According to Pelin and collaborators [45] graphene oxide is capable of penetrating human primary keratinocytes. Although no data are currently available about GQDs skin penetration, our data suggest that high doses of GQDs in constant contact with the skin results in considerable levels of GQDs skin penetration, causing severe consequences to circulatory system. According to Kenry [46], while graphene nanomaterials have huge bioapplications, they may also induce undesirable toxicity under certain conditions, specially at high doses.

The histopathological analysis showed that hamsters treated topically with GQDs at 0.5 g/Kg showed signs of mild panniculitis. A multifocal inflammatory infiltrate was identified in the hypodermis, mainly composed of polymorphonuclear cells (Fig. 5, B). The group treated topically with GQDs at 1 g/Kg had severe panniculitis (Fig. 5, C). The inflammatory infiltrate, which was also composed of polymorphonuclear cells, was diffusely distributed through the hypodermis, with few areas of steatonecrosis. The group treated i.p. with GQDs at 1 g/Kg had alterations in the carotid artery. The intimal layer in the treated group (Fig. 5, E) showed marked thickening when compared to the arteries in control animals (Fig. 5, D); the presence of inflammatory cells adhered to the vessel wall was also observed.

Some studies have been investigated significant hemorheological changes including hemolysis, aggregation, deformability of human erythrocytes exposed to nanoparticles. Nemmar and Coworkers [47] demostrated that erythrocytes exposed to silver nanoparticles at 400 µg/ml showed significant alterations due to hemolysis. Khan and collaborators [48] demonstrated hemolytic activity in human erythrocytes exposed to zinc oxide or titanium dioxide nanoparticles.

In consonance with the data about the damage caused by GQDs to microcirculation, we have observed that GQDs also showed hemolytic effect toward human erythrocytes (Fig. 6). A possible reason for mechanism involved in the hemolytic effect of the GQDs could be the generation of oxidative stress. GQDs move into the cytosol through the cell membrane and are accumulated in vesicles [49]. The high intracellular accumulation of GQDs and aggregation of the surface of cells results in an increase of oxidative stress levels causing deformation, leading to cell apoptosis and membrane destruction [48–49].

Our study demonstrated that graphene quantum dots affect the microcirculation in an animal model, causing a massive destruction of microvessels structures and hemolytic activity against human erythrocytes, a finding that seriously put into question the safety of these particles use in humans, especially at high doses. Further research is warranted to elucidate in more details the mechanisms underlying the microcirculatory damage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
ce:section.ce:para: All procedures were approved by State University of Rio de Janeiro Animal Care and Use Committee (Rio de Janeiro, RJ, Brazil; protocol number CEUA/021/2015), which is consistent with United States National Institute of Health Guide for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals (National Research Council, 1996).
All authors are in accordance with the publication of this material.
All data will be available under request.

This study was funded by Carlos Chagas Filho Foundation for Research Support of Rio de Janeiro State (FAPERJ) (Cientista do Nosso Estado: E-26/200.815/2021; Rede NanoSaude: E-26/0https://doi.org/10.000981/2019, Pesquisa na UEZO: E-26/0https://doi.org/10.002362/2019; Temáticos: E-26/211.269/2021, Infraestrutura e Pesquisa na UEZO e UERJ: E-26//211.207/2021, Bolsa de Pós Doutorado Senior (PDS): E-26/202.320/2021) CNPq (Bolsa de Produtividade 1B: 301069/2018-2) to Ralph Santos-Oliveira.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Angio-Based Index of Microcirculatory Resistance for the Assessment of the Coronary Resistance: A Proof of Concept Study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tebaldi M, Biscaglia S, Di Girolamo D, Erriquez A, Penzo C, Tumscitz C, Campo G. Angio-Based Index of Microcirculatory Resistance for the Assessment of the Coronary Resistance: A Proof of Concept Study. J Interv Cardiol. 2020 Oct 25;2020:8887369. doi: 10.1155/2020/8887369.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: From the Coronary to the Peripheral Microcirculation: Using a Validated Concept to Develop a Novel Index

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ziada KM, Shishehbor MH. From the Coronary to the Peripheral Microcirculation: Using a Validated Concept to Develop a Novel Index. JACC Cardiovasc Interv. 2020 Apr 27;13(8):986-988. doi: 10.1016/j.jcin.2019.12.024.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cerebral microcirculation in glioblastoma: A major determinant of diagnosis, resection, and drug delivery
